Gender Equality, Quality Education, and the Global Decline in Fertility Rates Chapter 1 Introduction

Gender Equality, Quality Education, and the Global Decline in Fertility Rates Graham Nystrom 2026-04-22 Chapter 1 Introduction Just ten years ago UN global leaders set an agenda to transform the world through a unified commitment to sustainable development. Their agenda lists 17 wide reaching and broadly scoped goals meant to provide the framework for a future of equitable global development centered on ecological sustainability and social justice. These Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), all of which are inextricably linked, aim to tackle extremely complex issues. By design, any concerted effort towards the progress of one SDG often also promotes progress or has implications for the status of another SDG. Two SDGs are so fundamentally tied that they become interdependent on one another. SDG #5, which aims to promote gender equality across the globe, is not attainable without progress made in SDG #4, which focuses on promoting quality education for all. What follows is a short contextualization of scholarly perspectives on the progress of these two SDGs that will provide background on the difficulties, progresses, and remaining challenges left to address in the pursuit of these two SDGs.

Chapter 2 SDG #4 Quality Education SDG #4 calls for “inclusive and equitable quality education” and lifelong learning for all (Unterhalter, 2019). Its targets span early childhood through adulthood and include the promotion of gender equality — connecting it directly to SDG #5. Despite decades of international commitment, real functional progress toward equitable and quality education in the developing world has been limited. Reports have found that nine in ten children in low-income countries cannot read with comprehension by age ten (Beeharry, 2021). The author argues that the problem is not in the lack of intention, but instead a failure to design and implement effective functional solutions. Without rigorous, system-wide effort, Beeharry warns, SDG #4’s target of twelve years of schooling for every girl by 2030 will not be met (2021). Unterhalter (2019) cautions that expanding educational access alone is insufficient. Education systems can reproduce the very inequalities they aim to address, particularly along lines of gender. Meaningful progress on SDG #4 therefore requires not just greater access to schooling, but a shift toward greater equitability in schooling systems — a distinction with direct consequences for women’s empowerment and progress towards SDG #5 (Unterhalter, 2019).

Chapter 3 SDG #5 Gender Equality Sustainable Development Goal #5 aims to empower women and girls by eliminating gender disparities, discrimination, and violence (Eden & Wagstaff, 2021). Gender equality, as Küfeoğlu (2022) describes it, is foundational to equitable development. Issues in gender equality include the elimination of gender-based violence, promoting equal pay, equal employment opportunity, and equal representation in education (Küfeoğlu, 2022). Progress remains slow and discriminatory laws and gender norms continue to restrict women’s access to education and careers globally (UNDESA, 2025). Women are paid roughly 16% less than men, and in approximately 90 countries women perform domestic and nursing labor without compensation. Statistics from 2017 show that 21% of women aged 20–24 had been married before age 18, limiting their educational and economic opportunities (Küfeoğlu, 2022). Eden and Wagstaff (2021) characterize SDG #5 as a “wicked problem” — one with no universal remedy, especially across diverse cultural contexts. Even so, they stress that complexity is not an excuse for inaction. Studies have shown that economic investment in developing nations can drive female empowerment, but only when paired with equitable education systems — pointing directly to the interdependence of SDGs #4 and #5 (Roy & Xiaoling, 2022).
Chapter 4 The Confluence of SDGs #4 and #5 — Fertility Rates as a Signal of Progress

Chapter 4 The Confluence of SDGs #4 and #5 — Fertility Rates as a Signal of Progress The SDG framework for measuring progress in equitable and sustainable development requires frequent progress assessments through data analysis. A notable demographic trend has accompanied progress on gender equality and education — global fertility rates are declining. Reports show a drop from roughly five children per woman in 1965 to about 2.5 today. Researchers attribute this shift primarily to three factors: women’s empowerment and access to education, declining child mortality, and the rising cost of raising children (Roser, 2014). The relationship between women’s education and lower fertility is well-established across many contexts. Jain (1981) identified this pattern across twelve countries, and Martín (1995) confirmed it across 26 demographic and health surveys, finding that higher education is consistently associated with lower fertility, later marriage, and greater contraceptive use — all indicators of women’s reproductive autonomy. Another study found the same inverse relationship in China, and proposed that declining fertility rates can themselves serve as a novel indicator of progress toward SDGs #4 and #5 (Chen & Guo, 2022). At the individual level, a recent article from the New York Times found that women in the United States are delaying childbearing not out of indifference, but out of a desire for financial stability and readiness: “I want to be financially stable and in a place I can call my own… I saw my parents get stressed over money, and I don’t want my kid to experience that.” (Tavernise & Adelson, 2026). This reflects a broader shift in more developed nations; women exercising the informed, autonomous decision-making that the SDG framework was designed to enable. The global decline in fertility rates is less a cause for concern than a sign that decades of investment in gender equality and education are beginning to take hold, particularly in more developed nations across the globe.
